title: Module
hook: Every NestJS project has 30 files and zero spaghetti — here's why.
analogy: A department in a company: owns its own staff and tools, formally requests what it needs from others, and chooses what to share.
goldenRule: One feature, one module — declare what you own, borrow, and lend.

title: Controller
hook: Your controller method has one job. If it has two, you've already broken it.
analogy: The receptionist at a doctor's clinic: takes your name, routes you to the right doctor, hands back the answer — never examines you.
goldenRule: A controller receives and delegates — zero business logic, zero database calls, ever.

title: Service
hook: You found where NestJS actually hides the code.
analogy: The doctor: examines the problem, applies business rules, prescribes a result — never answers phones or handles paperwork.
goldenRule: Services are the only layer that knows about business rules — if a service imports HTTP objects, it's in the wrong layer.

title: Provider
hook: What do a database wrapper, an email client, and a Stripe SDK have in common in NestJS?
analogy: Appliances plugged into a power grid: @Injectable() is the plug, the module's providers array connects it to the grid.
goldenRule: @Injectable() marks the plug — but the module's providers array is what connects it to the power.

title: Decorator
hook: That @ symbol isn't magic — it's a sticky label, and NestJS is the handler reading it.
analogy: Sticky labels on a file folder: URGENT, CONFIDENTIAL, ADMIN ONLY — the content doesn't change, the label tells the handler what to do.
goldenRule: Decorators attach metadata to classes, methods, and parameters — NestJS reads those labels at startup to wire the app.

title: Bootstrapping / main.ts
hook: Where does a NestJS app actually start? One function, five things, in this exact order.
analogy: The building manager on opening day: unlocks the doors, turns on the power, verifies permits, opens reception — then business starts.
goldenRule: main.ts wires global config once — ValidationPipe, CORS, port — before accepting a single request.

title: DTO
hook: One missing rule in a DTO and a client can inject any userId they want into your database.
analogy: A customs declaration form: undeclared items are confiscated, unknown items are detained, a malformed form gets turned back at the border.
goldenRule: Never accept id, tenantId, or userId as client-provided DTO fields — those are server-assigned values.

title: Pipe
hook: Raw request data is dirty. This is the layer that cleans it before it touches your handler.
analogy: A water purification system: raw tap water flows through, impurities are removed, only clean certified water reaches the handler.
goldenRule: Pipes transform or validate route arguments — they run after guards, before your handler executes.

title: Guard
hook: The first question every request must answer: are you who you claim to be? That's authentication. Guards are where NestJS answers it.
analogy: A nightclub bouncer: checks your wristband, checks the VIP section, checks the banned list — it's yes or no, no negotiation.
goldenRule: Guards return true or throw — the only correct place for authentication and authorisation logic.

title: Interceptor
hook: How do you run code both before and after a handler without touching the handler? With a sandwich.
analogy: A sandwich: top bread runs before the handler, the filling is the handler, bottom bread runs after — only complete when both slices are in place.
goldenRule: Interceptors wrap handlers via RxJS — use them for logging execution time, caching, and transforming response shape.

title: Middleware
hook: Middleware and Interceptors both run on every request. They are not the same thing.
analogy: The airport body scanner before check-in: no boarding pass checked yet, just makes sure nothing dangerous enters before the specific checks begin.
goldenRule: Middleware runs before NestJS processing — use it for correlation IDs, security headers, and rate limiting.

title: Exception Filter
hook: Without this, your API throws raw stack traces at your users.
analogy: The customer service desk: something went wrong on the shop floor, the desk catches it, packages it professionally, and hands back a clear structured response.
goldenRule: Exception Filters catch thrown errors and shape them into consistent, structured HTTP responses with correlation IDs and timestamps.

title: Dependency Injection
hook: If you're writing 'new EmailService()' inside a class, you've already made it impossible to test.
analogy: A professional staffing agency: the chef declares what staff they need, the agency delivers them on opening day — and sends stand-ins for the test kitchen.
goldenRule: Declare dependencies in the constructor — NestJS delivers instances automatically, and tests can swap any of them for mocks.

title: Scope
hook: Three scopes. Pick the wrong one and tenant A's data bleeds into tenant B's request.
analogy: Coffee service styles: DEFAULT is one communal pot for the whole office, REQUEST is a fresh cup per visitor, TRANSIENT is a new cup every time anyone reaches for one.
goldenRule: Most providers should be singletons — use REQUEST scope only for stateful per-request context like TenantContext.

title: Repository Pattern
hook: Services should never write TypeORM queries. Here's who should — and why it matters for testing.
analogy: A librarian: you say what you need, you don't go into the stacks yourself, you don't know how the filing system works — the librarian fetches and hands it back.
goldenRule: The repository is the only layer allowed to talk to the database — services ask, it fetches, and tests mock it without a real DB.

title: Entity
hook: Your database schema and your TypeScript type — one class, one source of truth.
analogy: A government form template: every filled-in form (database row) must match the template exactly, and revisions become new versions.
goldenRule: Never use synchronize: true in production — it can silently drop columns without a migration review.

title: TypeORM Migration
hook: There is one thing you should never do to your production database schema. Migrations are how you avoid it.
analogy: Git commits for your database: every commit is reversible, you can see the full history, and you never rewrite a past commit in production.
goldenRule: Every schema change is a migration with up() and down() — never alter a past migration, always add a new one.

title: ConfigModule / ConfigService
hook: A missing environment variable should crash your app at startup — not at 2am in production.
analogy: The official company policy handbook in a locked cabinet: one source, any employee can request a policy by name, and the office doesn't open until the handbook is complete.
goldenRule: Validate your .env schema at startup with Joi — if required variables are missing, the app refuses to start.

title: CQRS
hook: Your service method is 200 lines of mixed reads and writes. CQRS is how you split that apart for good.
analogy: A restaurant with two completely separate kitchens: the order kitchen changes state, the reading kitchen only describes what's available — a waiter from one cannot place orders in the other.
goldenRule: Commands change state, Queries read it — mixing them in one service method creates untestable God classes.

title: CommandBus / QueryBus
hook: Your resolver dispatches a command and has zero knowledge of which class handles it. That's the feature.
analogy: A national postal sorting facility: you write the letter, drop it in the slot, the facility routes it to the right driver — you never drive to the destination yourself.
goldenRule: The bus routes messages to handlers — callers and handlers are fully decoupled and the handler can be replaced without touching the caller.

title: EventBus / Domain Events
hook: Three things happen after a user signs up. None of them belong in the command handler.
analogy: A public PA announcement over the office: HR adds to payroll, IT provisions a laptop, the manager books induction — all independently, the PA operator manages none of it.
goldenRule: Domain events are past-tense facts — publish after success, and unrelated listeners react independently without the publisher knowing who they are.

title: Resolver (GraphQL)
hook: REST gives you five fixed lanes. GraphQL lets the client drive the shape of every response.
analogy: A personal shopper vs a traffic cop: the shopper fetches the exact combination you asked for in one trip, no over-fetching, no three separate API calls.
goldenRule: A Resolver is the GraphQL equivalent of a Controller — route to the CommandBus or QueryBus, nothing else.

title: Passport Strategy
hook: JWT for users, API keys for services, OAuth for Google — three mechanisms, one guard system.
analogy: Different ID verification lanes at the same border crossing: passport lane, work permit lane, biometrics lane — a passport doesn't get you through the work permit lane.
goldenRule: Each strategy is a named provider — guards call strategies by name to authenticate one specific mechanism.

title: JWT — HS256 vs RS256
hook: With HS256, one compromised microservice can forge tokens for every user in your system.
analogy: HS256 is a master key — anyone who has it can lock and unlock. RS256 is a royal wax seal — only the king has the signet ring, anyone can verify the seal but no one can forge it.
goldenRule: In multi-service systems, use RS256 — only the auth service holds the private key; downstream services can verify but never forge.

title: DataLoader & the N+1 Problem
hook: 100 posts, 100 author queries. That's not a bug — it's GraphQL working exactly as designed, and it will destroy your database.
analogy: A barista making one warehouse trip per cup vs one trip for the whole morning rush: 100 customers, 1 trip instead of 100.
goldenRule: DataLoader batches N lookups into 1 query — it must be REQUEST-scoped, never shared across requests.

title: Bull Queue
hook: Sending an email inside an HTTP handler is a timeout waiting to happen.
analogy: The ticket rail in a restaurant kitchen: the waiter clips the order and returns to the next table immediately — never stands next to the stove watching the steak cook.
goldenRule: Enqueue heavy work and return immediately — a separate worker process handles it asynchronously at its own pace.

title: Redis PubSub & GraphQL Subscriptions
hook: Your GraphQL subscription works perfectly in development — until you add a second server instance in production.
analogy: In-memory PubSub is a walkie-talkie: only people in direct radio range receive it. Redis PubSub is a broadcast tower: one station transmits, every radio in the country picks it up.
goldenRule: In-memory PubSub breaks with multiple servers — Redis PubSub fans events to every instance so all clients receive updates.

title: Multi-tenancy
hook: One app, one database, hundreds of customers — and none of them can see each other's data.
analogy: A cloud storage unit facility: all units share the same building and staff, but every operation must check the unit number first — an accidental open is a catastrophic data breach.
goldenRule: Shared tables + tenantId column is the simplest, most cost-efficient multi-tenancy strategy for B2B SaaS.

title: tenantId Pattern
hook: Your DTO is clean. Your tenantId is still in the wrong place.
analogy: The unit number stamped on everything: every domain row carries the unit number, and it only ever comes from the key issued at the door — never from the visitor's own claim.
goldenRule: tenantId comes only from the JWT — never accept it as a client-provided DTO field, never make it a filterable field.

title: TenantGuard & TenantContext
hook: How does tenantId get from the JWT to every service in a request without passing it through every function signature?
analogy: An automatic unit key issued at the entrance: the guard stamps it at the door, and every room in the building accepts it without you showing ID again.
goldenRule: TenantGuard extracts tenantId into a REQUEST-scoped TenantContext — inject it anywhere in the request chain, no threading required.

title: RBAC — Roles & RolesGuard
hook: You're authenticated. That doesn't mean you can do everything. RolesGuard answers the second question: are you allowed to do this specific thing?
analogy: The VIP wristband system: a standard wristband doesn't get you into the VIP section — the system checks the band, not your word.
goldenRule: @Roles() declares required roles on the handler, RolesGuard enforces them — two decorators, zero manual role checks in business logic.

title: Row-Level Authorization with @Authorize
hook: You have the right role. You still can't see another tenant's data. @Authorize is the only layer that enforces that at the database, not the application.
analogy: A turnstile built into the database corridor: even if someone slips past the front door, the turnstile still requires a valid badge to physically reach the data rows.
goldenRule: @Authorize merges a WHERE filter at the query builder level — it is architecturally impossible to bypass with a clever query.

title: Dual-Auth Architecture
hook: A user who adds role: 'admin' to their JWT payload still cannot access your admin portal. Here's the cryptographic reason.
analogy: Two separate key rings: the user ring and the admin ring are mathematically distinct — a key from one ring is physically incapable of opening the other's locks.
goldenRule: User and admin portals use different RSA key pairs — a user JWT is cryptographically invalid against the admin strategy regardless of its payload claims.

title: Nx Monorepo & Module Boundaries
hook: A frontend developer imports a backend class. ESLint catches it before CI even runs.
analogy: An apartment building with strict bylaws: tenants cannot enter each other's units, and the building's own alarm enforces it — not just the manager's word.
goldenRule: Nx enforces that apps cannot import each other directly — all code sharing goes through libs/, caught at the IDE level.

title: The Full Request Lifecycle
hook: Something's wrong in production. This is the map that tells you which layer to blame.
analogy: A building with mandatory checkpoints at every floor: you cannot skip a floor, and each checkpoint has one specific job.
goldenRule: Middleware → Guards → Pipes → Interceptors → Handler → Service → DB → Interceptors → Filter — know this order and you know where any bug lives.
